retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Pseudogenes play an essential role in comparative studies regarding genomics as they can provide a record of ancient genes. They are used to determine the rate of gene duplication and follow the evolution of sequence changes in organisms. Thus, pseudogenes are unique and helpful for phylogenetic studies.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Pseudogenes are located diffusely throughout the genome. Due to the sequence similarity between pseudogenes and their functional counterparts, it is often difficult for scientists as misidentification errors can and do occur frequently. Additionally, not every gene within the genome has associated pseudogenes. Some genes even have paralogous pseudogenes, that, with evolution, have been able to insert into different chromosomes from their functional gene copies. However, the identification of pseudogenes is significant and necessary to understand their molecular role in disease as well as their relationship to their functional gene copy.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, due to the diverse and vast amount of pseudogenes sharing similar coding sequences to their functional gene counterparts, there is no standard method for identifying them without identifying the entire genome. However, many independent groups are currently working to resolve this dilemma and create a standardized and efficient means of pseudogene identification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1330
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, each dedicated to a distinct type of chemical transformation. The subclass isomerases (isomerasy) catalyze the rearrangement of atoms within a molecule, converting it into its isomeric form. Ligases (ligasy) facilitate the joining of two molecules with the concomitant hydrolysis of a small pendant group such as ATP, forming new chemical bonds. Lyases (lyasy) break chemical bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, often generating a new double bond or a new ring structure. Oxidoreductases (oxidoreduktasy) mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, transferring electrons between substrates. Transferases (transferasy) move functional groups, such as methyl or phosphate groups, from one molecule to another. Together, these enzyme subclasses encompass the primary mechanisms by which cells accelerate and regulate metabolic pathways, underscoring their central role in physiology and therapeutic targeting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2786
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes, hematological are identified as members of both the deoxyribonuclease group and the trypsin group, indicating that these blood‑related enzymes are classified within two distinct enzyme families. As members of the deoxyribonuclease family, they possess nuclease activity capable of cleaving DNA, while their classification as members of the trypsin family reflects proteolytic functions typical of serine proteases. This dual categorization highlights the multifunctional nature of hematological enzymes, which can participate in both nucleic acid metabolism and protein digestion processes within the circulatory system, underscoring the importance of recognizing overlapping enzymatic roles in patient care and biomedical research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1892
